claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 18 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
circumstances: Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you find out if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

Let's start: **How many children are you enquiring about, and what are their names and ages?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! I'm looking into claiming Child Benefit. I have one child - my son Alex, who's 18 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information about Alex.

Since Alex is 18, there are specific conditions that need to be met for you to continue receiving Child Benefit for him. Let me ask a few more questions.

**Is Alex currently in approved education or training?** This would include things like:
- A-levels or equivalent
- A course at a college or training provider
- An apprenticeship (in some cases)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not in any education or training at the moment. He finished school and hasn't started anything else.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that.

I have one more question to help determine eligibility:

**Is Alex registered with a careers service, Connexions, or a similar organisation for training or work?** In England, this would mean being registered with a local careers service or training provider; in Scotland, Wales, or Northern Ireland, there are equivalent services.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he hasn't registered with any careers service or anything like that. He's just been at home since he left school.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Let me check the specific eligibility rules to make sure I give you accurate information about Alex's situation.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for your patience. Based on the information you've provided about Alex and the official eligibility rules, I now have enough information to give you a decision.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, I'm afraid **you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

Here's why:

- **Alex is 18 years old** – Child Benefit normally stops when a child turns 16, unless they stay in approved education or training.
- **Alex is not in approved education or training** – For children aged 16-19, Child Benefit can continue only if they remain in approved education or training (such as A-levels, college courses, etc.).
- **The careers service registration option doesn't apply** – There is an option to continue Child Benefit for 20 weeks if a child leaves education and registers with a careers service, but this only applies to **16 and 17 year olds**, not 18 year olds like Alex.

**What could change this:**
If Alex were to start approved education or training (such as a college course or certain vocational training), you may then become eligible to claim Child Benefit for him, as long as he is under 20 when he starts.

I hope this helps clarify things. Please feel free to ask if you have any other questions!

reasonings: Alex is 18 years old and has left school. For children aged 16-19, Child Benefit can only continue if the child stays in approved education or training. Alternatively, if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training, Child Benefit can continue for 20 weeks if they register with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services. Since Alex is 18 (not 16 or 17), this registration option does not apply to him. As Alex is not in approved education or training, and is too old to qualify through the careers service registration route, he does not meet the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex, who is 18 years old. Alex has finished school and is not currently in any approved education or training, nor is he registered with a careers service. Child Benefit for children over 16 requires them to be in approved education or training until age 20. The alternative route of registering with a careers service only applies to 16 and 17 year olds. Since Alex meets neither of these conditions, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for him.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 18 years old and has left school. For children aged 16-19, Child Benefit can only continue if the child stays in approved education or training. Alternatively, if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training, Child Benefit can continue for 20 weeks if they register with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services. Since Alex is 18 (not 16 or 17), this registration option does not apply to him. As Alex is not in approved education or training, and is too old to qualify through the careers service registration route, he does not meet the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex, who is 18 years old. Alex has finished school and is not currently in any approved education or training, nor is he registered with a careers service. Child Benefit for children over 16 requires them to be in approved education or training until age 20. The alternative route of registering with a careers service only applies to 16 and 17 year olds. Since Alex meets neither of these conditions, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for him.
